data.title: Sanlorenzo Target Started at EUR47 by UBS
Sanlorenzo Started at Buy by UBS
China to Impose Special Port Fees on U.S. Vessels
data.description:
China plans to impose a special port fee on U.S. vessels docking at Chinese ports, in retaliation for the Trump administration's move to levy fees on Chinese ships.

China's Ministry of Transportation said Friday the fees will be collected on vessels owned by U.S. companies, organizations, or individuals. Vessels with 25% or more of their equity owned by U.S. entities, as well as those flying the U.S. flag, will also be subject to the special port fee, it added.

These vessels docking at Chinese ports will be charged 400 yuan or $56 per net ton from October 14. The rate will rise to 640 yuan per net ton from April 17, 2026, 880 yuan from April 17, 2027, and 1,120 yuan from April 17, 2028, according to the ministry.

For vessels calling at multiple Chinese ports in the same voyage, the special port fee will only be collected at the first port of call, and each vessel will be subject to the special port fee for no more than five voyages per year.

The latest move by Beijing came in response to the Trump administration's decision to impose similar fees, charging built, owned, or operated by Chinese entities $80 per net ton per voyage to the U.S. Non-Chinese operators of Chinese-built ships will face the higher of two rates, either $23 per net ton or $154 per 20-foot-equivalent unit of capacity.

China's Ministry of Transportation said the U.S. move violates international trade principles and the China-U.S. maritime agreement, severely disrupting maritime trade between the two countries.

Write to singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: Russian debt may still pay out, but the clock is ticking: Weidemaier and Gulati
data.description:
By Mark Weidemaier and Mitu Gulati

Holders of Russian foreign debt surely wish U.S. President Donald Trump had fulfilled his promise to bring the war in Ukraine to a quick end, as payments on most instruments have been on hold since Russia’s invasion began. The clock for making claims may now be ticking.

Russian sovereign debt denominated in euros and U.S. dollars stopped paying out in April 2022 after Western powers slapped sanctions on Moscow. The longer the conflict drags on, the more bondholders might wonder whether they need to do anything to preserve their right to payment.

PRESCRIPTION PANIC?

Under normal circumstances, when a bond issuer fails to pay, the bondholder must file suit within the relevant statute of limitations. These statutes exist to protect both defendants and courts from stale claims.

However, in many jurisdictions, parties may agree to shorten these limitation periods. Russia’s bond contracts have so-called “prescription clauses” seemingly designed to do just that.

These clauses say that bondholder “claims” for principal and interest “shall become void” unless “made” within three years of the payment’s due date.

It has been well over three years since Russia’s first missed payments on April 4, 2022. Does this mean bondholder claims are starting to become void?

The answer may not be so obvious.

To begin, it is unclear what “making a claim” even means. How formal does this action need to be? What type of communication will suffice?

Courts are rarely called upon to interpret prescription clauses like the one used by Russia, so there is limited precedent to draw on.

There is some, however. In litigation against Argentina in 2022, a U.S. federal court in New York held that investors had made a claim against the country simply by asking it to enter an agreement to suspend the limitations period.

Russia’s bonds are governed by English – not New York law – but perhaps UK courts would take a similar approach.

Russia’s bonds are held via depository institutions that reportedly send automated reminders of payment due dates to the issuer. Might that form of communication be enough?

Many legal experts are skeptical, but the limited case law here leaves open the possibility.

EXERCISE IN FUTILITY

Russian bondholders could reasonably make the argument that, given sanctions, they ought to be excused for failing to “make” their claims within the prescription period.

After all, even if bondholders had taken legal action and received a favorable judgment in English courts, they would have had little hope of enforcing it against frozen Russian assets, especially given that Russian bonds do not include the waivers of sovereign immunity typically found in government bonds.

Meanwhile, the Russian government has publicly maintained that it intends to pay once sanctions are lifted.

Given both the futility of filing a lawsuit and the government’s apparent willingness to pay, what would be the point of requiring bondholders to undertake formal steps to “make” claims for missed payments?

Still, the text of the prescription clause seems clear. Regardless of whether it is sensible, “making” a claim is required within three years, and bondholders didn’t do that.

EXTENUATING CIRCUMSTANCES

Not so fast though. The presence of an ongoing war complicates matters further.

There are several old U.S. cases in which courts ruled that the statute of limitations could be extended during a period of war.

The logic of these cases seems to have been that the war effectively denied creditors access to the courts. That logic could potentially be extended to contractual prescription periods, when war-related sanctions make payments essentially impossible.

But even if that’s the case and Russia resumes making payments, including catching up on missed payments, after sanctions are eventually lifted, there will still be more questions.

Ordinarily, if there is an extended delay in making payments, the debtor will owe additional interest for the delay period. This is rarely disputed, and the arguments tend to be over what interest rate pertains to the delay period.

There are, once again, old cases related to claims made during war time that are relevant here, but this time, they aren’t in creditors’ favor. These cases typically hold that interest does not accrue during the period of delay when the cause of the interruption is a war.

For a variety of reasons, the logic of these cases may not apply here.

For one, Russia is clearly the aggressor in this war and thus is responsible for its current inability to pay, given that it could restore this ability simply by ceasing its aggression.

Under those circumstances, many would think it perverse to deny investors interest while they wait. Still, the law here is not as clear on this point as many investors might assume.

Either way, with the war likely to drag into its fourth year, Russia’s willingness to pay foreign bondholders may be diminishing. Holders of Russian debt may therefore soon determine that the risk of doing nothing – and banking on Moscow’s eventual compliance – is just too high.

(The views expressed here are those of the authors, Mark Weidemaier and Mitu Gulati, professors of law at the Universities of North Carolina and Virginia, respectively. They host the podcast, Clauses and Controversies, where they speculate about fun sovereign debt contract matters).

Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), your essential new source for global financial commentary. ROI delivers thought-provoking, data-driven analysis of everything from swap rates to soybeans. Markets are moving faster than ever. ROI, can help you keep up. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, and X.

data.title: Japan's Ruling Party Loses Ally in Fresh Political Shake-Up — Market Talk
data.description: The surprise exit of a longstanding ally of Japan's ruling party clouds an already murky outlook for the country, which is in the midst of a leadership transition. The Komeito party has severed a decades-long relationship with the Liberal Democratic Party, citing a disagreement over political funding issues. That may throw a spanner in the works for the LDP's newly minted leader, Sanae Takaichi, and her efforts to become the next prime minister. Takaichi will need to gain approval in parliament to be named as Japan's first-ever female premier, and Komeito's exit could well raise that hurdle. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

data.title: Ferretti Target Started at EUR4.35 by UBS
Ferretti Started at Buy by UBS
FTSE Moves Further Away from Record High
data.description:
The FTSE 100 traded lower on Friday, extending losses from its record high of 9,549 on October 8, as weakness in mining and energy stocks weighed on the index.

Precious metals miners led declines, with Endeavour Mining down 3.7% and Fresnillo losing over 3.5% as gold prices paused after recent gains.

Rio Tinto and Anglo American fell about 1%, while Glencore and Antofagasta eased 0.5% as copper prices pulled back.

Energy majors Shell (-0.6%) and BP (-0.3%) followed oil lower.

The index was also dragged by heavyweight overseas earners, including AstraZeneca (-0.8%) and HSBC (-0.5%), as the dollar weakened.

Despite Friday’s dip, the FTSE 100 remains slightly higher for the week, poised to notch its third consecutive weekly gain.

data.title: Seatrium's Potential Contract Loss Likely Priced In — Market Talk
data.description: The possible termination of a US$475 million contract has likely been priced into Seatrium's shares, says Macquarie Capital Securities' Zhiwei Foo in a note. The terminated was probably linked to challenges in the U.S. offshore wind sector, including the Trump administration's crackdown on such projects, he says. Shares had fallen about 6% at the time of Foo's analysis, suggesting the market has already priced in a full write-off of the contract. While "the negatives appear priced in, we think the market is starting to question if there are any other negative surprises in the [order book]," he says. Nonetheless, Macquarie Capital maintains its outperform rating and S$2.80 target, citing Seatrium's S$18.6 billion order book. Shares are 7.4% lower at S$2.26.(megan.cheah@wsj.com)

data.title: flatexDEGIRO Target Started at EUR36.50 by UBS
flatexDEGIRO Started at Buy by UBS
Leonardo Well on Track to Meet Full-Year Guidance — Market Talk
data.description: Leonardo's third-quarter results, due for release on Nov. 5, will show that the Italian defense and aerospace group is well on track to meet its full-year guidance, Jefferies analysts Chloe Lemarie and Ben Brown say. They expect the company to post 5% revenue growth and 13% Ebita growth. The helicopters and aircraft businesses should continue to grow solidly, while electronics is expected to remain broadly flat on year, they forecast. Aerostructures and space should post higher volumes, they add. Leonardo might give investors an update on its strategy for mergers and acquisitions, but will probably keep any firm announcements for its full-year results call, the analysts say. Shares are down 3.5% at 53.54 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: REG - Bezant Resources PLC - Blackstone Investment - Mankayan Project Update
data.description:
RNS Number : 9110C Bezant Resources PLC 10 October 2025  

10 October 2025

Bezant Resources Plc

("Bezant")

Mankayan Project Update

Blackstone Minerals Investment

Bezant (AIM: BZT), the copper-gold exploration and development company, notes that ASX listed Blackstone Minerals Ltd ("Blackstone") has on 10 October 2025 announced, inter alia, that it has achieved several significant milestones as Blackstone advances its exploration and development strategy at the world class Mankayan Copper- Gold porphyry project in northern Luzon, Philippines.

As per the Company's announcement on 2 October 2025 Bezant's shareholding of Blackstone shares is now 80,574,880 Blackstone shares.

Start of key highlights announced by Blackstone (the "Company")

·     Two year work programme approved: Blackstone has secured regulatory support from the Philippine government. The Mines and Geosciences Bureau (MGB) has approved a two-year extension of the Mankayan Project's work program, under the existing Mineral Production Sharing Agreement  (MPSA). This extension allows Blackstone to continue critical exploration and study activities to maximise the potential of this globally significant asset.

·     Drill rug drill rig mobilised to site: The Company's drill rig has arrived on site and is currently being commissioned.

·     Land and community access secured:  Land access for the initial drill holes has been secured and pad and access road construction has commenced with community engagement underway.  In parallel to the land access agreement a Community Access agreement with Maggangan village in the Barangay of Guinaoang has been signed. The Company has committed to numerous community projects that will be delivered over a 2 year period.

·     NCIP certificate of precondition granted: The National Commission on Indigenous Peoples (NCIP) has issued its Certificate of Precondition attesting that Free, Prior and Informed Consent (FPIC) has been obtained from the Mankayan Indigenous Peoples. This certificate formally ratifies the memorandum of agreement (MoA) signed in December 2024, completing Blackstone social licence to operate. It is a historic first - Blackstone (in conjunction with Crescent Mining & Development Corp (CMDC) is the first mining company to obtain indigenous consent in the Mankayan region, underscoring the significance of the achievement for both the company and the local community.

·     Registered office to be established in Mankatyan:  The Company has initiated the process of relocating CMDC's registered office to Mankayan, in line with its commitment under the MoA.  This move underscores the Company's dedication to ensuring that the Mankayan community directly benefits from their natural resources.  The initiative has been possibly received by the Mankayan Municipal Government.

End of key highlights announced by Blackstone

Below is a link to the Blackstone announcement;

10 October 2025 titled "Mankayan Copper Gol Project - Work Program Approved" is at

https://announcements.asx.com.au/asxpdf/20251010/pdf/06qc6b2xqjgnz2.pdf 

For further information, please contact:

Bezant Resources Plc 

Colin Bird Executive Chairman

+44 (0) 20 3416 3695

Beaumont Cornish (Nominated Adviser) 

Roland Cornish / Asia Szusciak

or visit http://www.bezantresources.com

Beaumont Cornish's responsibilities as the Company's Nominated Adviser, including a responsibility to advise and guide the Company on its responsibilities under the AIM Rules for Companies and AIM Rules for Nominated Advisers, are owed solely to the London Stock Exchange. Beaumont Cornish is not acting for and will not be responsible to any other persons for providing protections afforded to customers of Beaumont Cornish nor for advising them in relation to the proposed arrangements described in this announcement or any matter referred to in it.

data.title: CAC 40 Edges Up as Banks and Automakers Lead Gains
data.description:
The Paris CAC 40 rose 0.2% to around 8,063 on Friday, recovering from mild losses in the previous session as investors remained cautious amid ongoing political uncertainty in France.

Market attention stayed focused on the evolving political landscape, with President Emmanuel Macron expected to appoint a new prime minister soon—his sixth leadership change since taking office.

Bank stocks outperformed, with BNP Paribas climbing 1.2%, Crédit Agricole adding 0.9%, and Société Générale advancing 0.5%.

In the industrial sector, Stellantis gained 1.8% after reporting a 13% year-on-year increase in global vehicle shipments in the third quarter.

Airbus edged up 0.1%, supported by projections for steady long-term growth in global aviation demand, expected to rise 10% in 2025 and reach $311 billion by 2044.

On the downside, ArcelorMittal led losses with a 2.6% drop, while TotalEnergies and Thales each slipped 0.5%.

data.title: Key facts: TSMC Q3 revenue up 30%; Barclays raises price target; scrutiny on Huawei AI chip
data.description:
• Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (2330) saw a 30% year-over-year revenue rise in Q3, hitting NT$989.9 billion ($32.5 billion), fueled by strong AI chip demand and major client orders.123
  • Barclays has maintained an 'Overweight' rating for Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (2330) and increased its price target from $325.00 to $330.00 per share.4
  • TechInsights found a TSMC chip in a Huawei AI processor, attracting scrutiny from China's commerce ministry and resulting in TechInsights being added to China's 'unreliable entity list.'5

data.title: India proposes to open up power retail sector to private cos, draft bill shows
data.description:
India plans to open up its electricity retail market for private companies, ending the dominance of state-run distributors in some states, according to draft regulations proposed by the federal power ministry.

The move will allow private companies such as Adani Power , Tata Power , Torrent Power and CESC to strengthen their presence in the country.

data.title: Everyone Missed Mastercard's Moat. Here's Why It Still Wins.
data.description:
In a world where capital chases the latest AI breakthroughs, social media platforms, or biotech moonshots, it's easy to overlook the companies that quietly sit at the center of global commerce. Mastercard is one of them. It's not trying to cure cancer or build the metaverse. It doesn't make chips or electric vehicles. Yet, it powers trillions of dollars in payments every single year, taking a small slice of each transaction. That tiny toll, scaled over billions of swipes, taps, and online checkouts, compounds into staggering shareholder returns.

This isn't a turnaround story. It isn't misunderstood. And it isn't cheap. But it is one of the cleanest expressions of a global monopoly in finance. For long-term investors, Mastercard has a fortress business model where scale, brand, and entrenched infrastructure create decades of compounding.

1. The Network That Runs Itself

Mastercard's business looks deceptively simple on the surface. It connects banks, merchants, and consumers, allowing money to move across borders and currencies in real time. But underneath that simplicity is one of the most defensible economic models in the world. Mastercard doesn't lend money. It doesn't carry credit risk. It's a pure toll collector, sitting in between issuers (the banks that give you cards) and acquirers (the banks or processors that connect merchants). For every transaction, it takes a small percentage, regardless of whether the consumer pays off the card balance, defaults, or carries debt.

That distinction is critical. Unlike banks, which swing with credit cycles, or buy-now-pay-later players, which are exposed to underwriting risk, Mastercard enjoys steady revenue growth tied to transaction volumes. It is asset-light, high-margin, and structurally resilient. Even in recessions, when spending slows, Mastercard's revenue base is cushioned by its global diversification and the fact that consumers still spend on essentials.

Once the pipes are built, they don't need to be rebuilt. The network reinforces itself. Every new merchant that joins Mastercard makes the card more valuable to consumers, and every new consumer that carries a Mastercard makes the network more valuable to merchants. It's the classic flywheel of a two-sided platform one that, after decades of compounding, is almost impossible to disrupt.

2. The Oligopoly That Prints Cash

Payments is one of the rare industries where competition looks fierce from the outside but is effectively an oligopoly inside. Visa and Mastercard dominate global card payments, with American Express holding niche share. Together, Visa and Mastercard control roughly 90% of the world's card volume outside China.

The beauty of this duopoly is that competition doesn't erode pricing. Instead of racing to the bottom, both networks have steadily increased their fees over decades. Merchants complain, regulators sometimes step in, but the basic economics remain intact. If you're Walmart (WMT) or Amazon (AMZN), you cannot realistically refuse to accept Mastercard without alienating customers. The cards are embedded into daily life, ingrained into consumer behavior, and stitched into the financial system itself.

This duopoly also translates into extraordinary profitability. Mastercard runs at operating margins over 58% numbers that rival software giants. The return on invested capital is super-high, fluctuating in the range of 37%-65% in the past decade, because it requires so little tangible capital to run the business. It doesn't need to pour billions into factories or inventory. The infrastructure is digital and global. Every incremental transaction flows through the same pipes with negligible incremental cost.

That is the definition of operating leverage. As the world keeps spending more on cards, Mastercard converts that growth into expanding margins and outsized earnings growth.

3. Growth Engines Beyond Plastic

The obvious bear case on Mastercard is that plastic cards will eventually fade, replaced by digital wallets, real-time payments, or decentralized finance. If consumers shift to Apple Pay , PayPal (PYPL), or central bank digital currencies, do Mastercard and Visa become obsolete toll collectors clinging to the past?

The answer lies in understanding that Mastercard is not a plastic card company. It's a network. Whether you swipe a physical card, tap your phone, or click a checkout button online, the transaction still runs through Mastercard's rails. Apple Pay and Google Pay may be the front end, but the back end is still Mastercard. Digital wallets are wrappers, not replacements.

Moreover, Mastercard has been proactive in expanding into new flows beyond consumer cards. Business-to-business (B2B) payments, cross-border remittances, government disbursements, and real-time account-to-account payments are all areas where Mastercard is positioning itself. These markets are measured in trillions and remain underpenetrated by digital solutions. B2B alone is a $120 trillion global market, and Mastercard's strategy is to capture even a small slice of that with specialized solutions for corporates.

The math tells the story. Mastercard processed $9.8 trillion in payments in 2024. If it captures just 1% of the $120 trillion B2B market, that's $1.2 trillion in new volumea 12% boost to its base. Even at conservative take rates, this adds high-margin revenue without stretching the business model. More importantly, it extends Mastercard's double-digit growth runway well into the next decade. With B2B still largely analog and Mastercard building rails into ERPs, supplier portals, and AP workflows, even 23% penetration over time isn't a stretch. That's how this compounding story keeps goingquietly, predictably, and at scale.

Cross-border remains another massive lever. Whenever money moves from one currency to another a tourist in Paris paying with a U.S. card, or a small business in Mexico selling to a customer in Germany Mastercard takes a higher cut. These transactions carry richer yields than domestic swipes, and they grow with globalization and e-commerce. Even after COVID disruptions, cross-border volumes are now higher than pre-pandemic levels, and as travel keeps rebounding, Mastercard rides the wave.

4. The Regulatory Overhang

The biggest risks to Mastercard don't come from rivals but from governments. Antitrust scrutiny is permanent. Regulators in the U.S., Europe, and emerging markets have long targeted interchange fees, alleging that Mastercard and Visa overcharge merchants and stifle competition. Periodically, fines are levied, rules are imposed, and lobbying intensifies.

Yet, history shows that regulation trims excesses but rarely breaks the model. Fee caps in Europe did not derail the networks. Antitrust lawsuits have dragged for years without upending the basic economics. Mastercard adapts, tweaks its pricing, or shifts costs elsewhere in the system. The structural need for a trusted, universal payment network outweighs the political appetite to dismantle it.

What does loom larger is the rise of government-backed real-time payment systems. India's UPI system has grown rapidly, eating into card volumes by offering free peer-to-peer and merchant payments. Brazil's PIX is another case where central bank-driven innovation has bypassed card rails. Mastercard faces the challenge of either coexisting with or embedding itself into these systems. In some cases, it has leaned into partnerships, offering fraud protection, identity verification, and cross-border linkages. It is betting that even if governments own the rails, Mastercard can still provide the high-value services layered on top.

5. The Financial Engine

If you stripped away the logos and just studied the numbers, you'd mistake Mastercard for a luxury software business disguised as a payments network. This is a capital-light machine that's been compounding at elite levels for over a decadeand shows no signs of slowing down.

Top-line growth has been remarkably consistent. Over the last ten years, Mastercard has grown revenue from $9.44 billion in 2014 to over $28.17 billion in 2024, a compound annual growth rate of over 11.5%. That's not some temporary COVID recovery spikeit's the result of a structurally advantaged model riding secular tailwinds. Even in 2020, when cross-border volumes fell off a cliff due to global lockdowns, Mastercard remained profitable and bounced back with force. But revenue is just the surface. The real story is told in margins. Operating margins consistently hover between 5364%, even after absorbing compliance costs, regulatory headwinds, and technology investments.

This isn't margin fat from underinvestment either. Mastercard plows serious cash back into product innovation, fraud prevention, tokenization infrastructure, cybersecurity, and its growing services business. In 2024, their valued-added services and solutions account for more than 38% of the revenue. As the core payment rails mature, Mastercard is evolving into a data-and-intelligence ecosystem that monetizes transaction flows in ways most competitors can't match. That services mix provides not just higher margins, but more resilience through economic cycles.

Free cash flow is just as pristine. In 2024, Mastercard generated $14.3 billion in free cash flownearly 51% of revenue. Over the past decade, free cash flow margins have routinely clocked in between 34%50%. That's what happens when you run a toll road that doesn't have to repave anything. Capex needs are minimal. And the company doesn't carry massive inventories or receivables that suck up cash. This is the textbook definition of capital-light.

Mastercard's return on invested capital is in a class of its own. Over the past decade, ROIC has ranged between 37% and 65%, a level of consistency that places it among the elite few in the S&P 500. Visa is the only real peer operating at that altitude. With returns that high, Mastercard doesn't need to chase reinvestment to compound shareholder value. It simply doesn't require much incremental capital to grow. Instead, management has steadily returned excess cash to shareholdersreducing the share count by more than 20% since 2013, while still compounding earnings per share at 16% annually. The dividend has followed the same disciplined path, growing at an 18.8% CAGR over the past ten years. It's a rare combination: high returns, capital-light operations, and a shareholder distribution strategy that doesn't compromise future growth.

The scale of that distribution is striking. In 2024, Mastercard repurchased $11.2 billion of stock and paid out $2.45 billion in dividends$13.65 billion in total cash returned to shareholders. On today's market cap, that equates to a 2.6% shareholder yield. At first glance that may seem modest, but it's layered on top of a business already compounding EPS at 16% annually over the past decade. Buybacks here aren't just financial engineeringthey're accelerants to an already capital-light compounding machine.

What makes all this more durable is the dual-pronged revenue engine that's still firing. First, there's volume growth. As the world shifts from cash to cards and from offline to online, Mastercard continues to process more gross dollar volume (GDV) year after year. In 2024, total GDV hit $9.8 trillion, growing 12.2% on a currency-neutral basis. That's not just inflationit's real volume expansion as contactless, mobile, and embedded payments penetrate every layer of daily life.

Second, there's pricing power. Mastercard doesn't just make money on volumeit clips a small fee on each transaction via assessment and cross-border fees, and earns authorization and switching fees from issuers and acquirers. That gives it leverage on both inflation and behavioral shifts. Every time a transaction migrates from cash to card, Mastercard earns a higher take. Every time a traveler swipes their card abroad, the company earns a higher-margin fee. Every time a fintech integrates with Mastercard's APIs or buys services from the company's Cyber & Intelligence unit, incremental revenue rolls in with little incremental cost.

And perhaps the most underappreciated lever is the company's expansion in emerging markets. Mastercard has made a quiet habit of entering underbanked regions through partnerships and minority investmentswhether that's buying stakes in African mobile payments networks, integrating with India's UPI system, or launching services for gig workers in Southeast Asia. These markets are still 80%+ cash. The long runway ahead is real, and Mastercard is positioning itself as the bridge between governments, banks, telcos, and merchants.

All of this points to one truth: Mastercard is still early in its long compounding runway. This isn't just a business growing fastit's one growing predictably, durably, and at high returns on capital without needing to dilute or overbuild. That's the engine we're buying.

6. Valuation: What Are We Really Paying For the Toll Road?

When a business generates over 50% free cash flow margins, earns returns on invested capital as high as 65%, and grows both revenue and earnings at a double-digit clip for more than a decade, it rarely comes cheap. Mastercard is no exception. On traditional multiples, it has seldom looked like a bargain. But for investors seeking to own a dominant network for the next 1020 years, sticker price tells only part of the story.

At today's levels, Mastercard trades at roughly 33x forward earnings and a similar multiple on next year's expected free cash flow. That looks expensive to many value-oriented investors. But what's being purchased isn't just a payment processor. It's a global duopoly with near-monopoly economics, structural tailwinds, and a capital-light model that reinvests at 20%+ returns without needing incremental assets.

History reinforces this point. Over the past fifteen years, Mastercard's forward P/E has been fluctuating in the range of 22.8x to nearly 49.2x. Currently it's staying at the 10-year mean valuation. Investors have almost never had the chance to buy it really cheap. Yet total returns have compounded at over 20% annually, not because the multiple expanded, but because the business delivered: consistent double-digit revenue growth, resilient 58%+ margins, and disciplined capital returns. The valuation looks demanding in the moment, but the stability of that band shows the market has long understood what this toll road is worth. Expensive? Always. Undervalued? Rarely.

Suppose Mastercard compounds free cash flow at 15% per year for the next decadea conservative pace, given its historical trajectory and the runway still available in emerging markets. By 2035, FCF would reach nearly $58 billion. Assigning a 25x multiple to that terminal figure suggests an enterprise value of around $1.45 trillion. Compared to today's market cap of $526 billion, that equates to a 10.7% annualized returneven after assuming some multiple compression along the way. That estimate doesn't factor in the potential impact of continued share repurchases or incremental margin gains.

A comparison with Visa helps frame expectations. Visa remains slightly larger in market cap and trades at a marginally lower valuation. But Mastercard has been growing faster and has taken a more deliberate path in expanding its servicesparticularly in data analytics, security, and advisory. It has also shown greater urgency in building partnerships and executing acquisitions in high-growth markets. While both companies generate excellent returns, Visa's ROIC has typically ranged from 13% to 35%, whereas Mastercard has maintained tighter and structurally higher returns.

That qualitative edge becomes even clearer when you line up the numbers side by side. Here's how Mastercard stacks up against Visa and AmExthe only real peers in the global payments arena.

Over a 20-year horizon, the gap in capital efficiency and growth optionality could begin to compound. Both businesses are best-in-class, but Mastercard may have slightly more levers left to pullwithout straying from its core strategy. If an investor had to pick one to own through multiple cycles, either would be defensible. But Mastercard's ability to compound from a leaner base, with broader reinvestment avenues, tilts the balance in its favor.

Ultimately, the more useful question isn't whether Mastercard is cheap by today's standards. It's whether the market fully appreciates the durability of its moatand the long tail of value creation that comes from reinvesting at structurally high returns. The price tag reflects more than today's earnings. It reflects ownership of a compounding engine that needs no reinvention, no strategic pivot, and no leverage to keep growing. There's no need to disrupt anything. Mastercard just needs to keep processing more payments, clipping fees, and expanding its services layer around the core infrastructure.

In a market filled with capital-intensive story stocks, Mastercard is the rare business that appears expensive at first glance, yet becomes more attractively priced the further out you zoom. If the market continues to value it like a mature payment processor while it performs like a SaaS-adjacent toll road, long-term owners are likely to be rewarded. And that's the bet.

7. The Moat That Endures

When you study great compounders, the common denominator is a moat that not only protects returns but also deepens with time. Every new swipe strengthens the moat. Every new merchant makes it harder for competitors to replicate. Every new consumer reinforces the ubiquity. The flywheel is self-reinforcing in a way that is nearly impossible to disrupt without government force.

The company has also proven remarkably adaptable. From magnetic stripes to chips to contactless to digital wallets, it has absorbed every technological shift without losing relevance. The risk is not zero real-time payments and crypto remain wild cards but the historical evidence suggests that Mastercard bends without breaking.

8. The Long Run

So where does Mastercard go from here? The bull case is straightforward. Global consumption keeps growing. Cash keeps declining. E-commerce keeps rising. Cross-border flows expand. B2B digitizes. All of these are secular megatrends measured in trillions. Mastercard doesn't need to win them all. It just needs to keep clipping its toll from a fraction of each.

The bear case hinges on disruption from new rails or government systems. If central banks push free real-time payments aggressively, if merchants rally against fees, or if Big Tech successfully bypasses the networks, Mastercard's economics could compress. Yet, these scenarios require coordination, regulatory will, and technological shifts that historically take decades. In the meantime, Mastercard continues to throw off billions in free cash every year, buying back stock and widening its moat.

This is not a stock for traders. It doesn't spike on product launches or quarterly surprises. It is a stock for compounding. The kind of business you buy and hold for decades, letting the flywheel of global commerce work for you.

Mastercard will never be the cheapest stock in your portfolio. But it might end up being one of the most reliable.

data.title: Azelis Group Target Cut to EUR12.50 From EUR17 by UBS
Azelis Group Cut to Neutral From Buy by UBS
UK's Ibstock warns of profit drop amid construction market woes
data.description:
Bricks and concrete maker Ibstock warned on Friday that its annual profit would fall below expectations amid persistent weakness in UK construction, sending shares down as much as nearly 15% in early trading.

The profit warning underscores growing challenges for construction material suppliers in the UK, as builders pull back amid economic uncertainty. The sector is already grappling with weak activity in residential construction.

The Leicestershire-based company said its clay and concrete segment's revenues were hit in the third quarter, and sales volumes in the second half of the year will be in line with those clocked in the first half.

"With clear, long-term structural imperatives for residential construction growth, it is disappointing that additional near-term headwinds are impacting momentum in our markets in the latter part of the year", CEO Joe Hudson said in a statement.

It expects second-half core profit to be similar to the first half's 35.5 million pounds ($47.17 million), as cautious customers in an uncertain economy limit sales volumes and its ability to raise prices.

The firm had previously guided core profits to be in the range of 77 million pounds to 82 million pounds for 2025.

British construction activity shrank for a ninth straight month in September as firms held off on major investments ahead of November's budget, a business survey showed.

"It has been a tough year, with a sluggish new housing market at the root of the volume softness" Peel Hunt analysts said, adding that general budget uncertainty was not helping matters.

($1 = 0.7527 pounds)

data.title: Malaysia announces budget spending of almost $100 billion in 2026, vows more reforms
data.description:
Key points:
  • Malaysia to spend 419 bln rgt in 2026, govt report says
  • Fiscal deficit seen at 3.5% of GDP in 2026
  • Growth seen at 4%-4.5% in 2026 vs 4%-4.8% in 2025
  • Petronas dividends to drop to 20 bln rgt in 2026

By Rozanna Latiff and Danial Azhar

Malaysia on Friday proposed a budget of 419.2 billion ringgit ($99.24 billion) for 2026, and vowed to pursue further fiscal reforms amid external uncertainties, according to government reports.

The Southeast Asian country has maintained steady growth even though its exports have been hit by changes in U.S. tariffs, but it needs to boost revenues to reduce its deficit and pursue the economic development goals unveiled in a five-year plan in July.

Since taking power in 2022, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim has introduced measures to bolster the government's coffers, including a minimum wage hike, an expanded sales tax and the removing of petrol and diesel subsidies for some segments of the population.

"It is only responsible for the government to carry out these reforms especially at a time when fiscal discipline is critical to navigating rising external risks," Anwar said in a foreword to the government's fiscal outlook report.

Anwar is set to address parliament at 4 p.m. (0800 GMT) when he will disclose more details of the budget.

The 2026 spending, up 1.7% on this year's revised 412.1 billion ringgit, includes development expenditure of 81 billion ringgit and operating expenditure of 338.2 billion ringgit.

ECONOMY COOLS ON TARIFF UNCERTAINTY

The government said it was on track to narrow its fiscal deficit to 3.5% of gross domestic product next year, from an estimated 3.8% in 2025.

Revenue is seen rising by 2.7% to 343.1 billion ringgit in 2026, from a projected 334.1 billion ringgit this year, according to fiscal and economic outlook reports released alongside Friday's budget.

State energy firm Petronas , a significant contributor to government revenues, will pay the government a dividend of 20 billion ringgit in 2026, its lowest since 2017, in anticipation of moderating crude oil prices and lower petroleum-related output and revenue.

Spending on subsidies and social assistance is projected to fall by 14.1% to 49 billion ringgit in 2026 from 57.1 billion ringgit this year, due to lower commodity prices and the government's efforts to deliver more targeted aid, the reports said.

Economic growth is forecast at 4% to 4.5% in 2026. This year's growth forecast was lowered to between 4% to 4.8% from an initial estimate of 4.5% to 5.5% in July, due to trade and tariff uncertainties. The United States has imposed a 19% tariff on most of Malaysia's exports to the country.

Malaysia's headline inflation is projected to remain manageable next year at between 1.3% to 2%, from a revised estimate of 1% to 2% in 2025, the government said.

Despite global market volatility from ongoing tariff tensions and geopolitical risks, the government said Malaysia's monetary policy remains supportive of the economy and would stimulate growth amid stable domestic prices.

Bank Negara Malaysia kept its benchmark interest rate at 2.75% last month, after cutting it for the first time in five years in July, with most analysts expecting rates to hold until the end of the year.

($1 = 4.2240 ringgit)

data.title: SEPTEMBER VOLUMES: MIXED PICTURE FOR FREIGHT
data.description:
INVESTOR NEWS no. 30 - 10 October 2025

Ferry - freight: Total volumes in September 2025 of 3.5m lane metres were 0.8%above 2024 and 1.2% lower adjusted for route changes. YTD growth rates were0.2% and -1.2%, respectively.

North Sea volumes were on level with 2024 following stable volumes on mostroutes. Mediterranean volumes were below 2024 following a continued slowdowninTurkish automotive manufacturing and domestic demand as well as reducedcapacityon one route in response to the competitive situation.

Channel volumes were above 2024 driven by the new Jersey routes. Baltic Seavolumes continued to be above 2024 and Strait of Gibraltar volumes were alsoabove 2024.

For the last twelve months 2025-24, the total transported freight lane metresincreased 2.3% to 41.7m from 40.7m in 2024-23. The increase was 1.2% adjustedfor route changes.

Ferry - passenger: The number of passengers in September 2025 was adjusted forroute changes* down 7.4% to 392k vs 2024 and the adjusted YTD growth ratewas -4.1%. Higher passenger volumes in Baltic Sea were offset by mainly lowerChannelvolumes. The number of cars in September was 5.7% below 2024 adjusted forroutechanges.

For the last twelve months 2025-24, the total number of passengersdecreased 14.5% to 5.5m compared to 6.4m for 2024-23. The growth rate was 0.4%adjusted for route changes.

*Adjusted for sale of Oslo-Frederikshavn-Copenhagen end October 2025, exitfromTarifa-Tanger Ville early May 2025, and addition of Jersey routes from endMarch2025.

DFDS ferry volumes

September   Last-12-months-------------------------------------------- ---------------------------- Freight 2023 2024 2025 Change   2023 2024 2025 Change

Lane metres, '000 3,320 3,475 3,502 0.8%   38,512 40,748 41,667 2.3%-------------------------------------------- ----------------------------

Passenger 2023 2024 2025 Change   2023 2024 2025 Change

Passengers, '000 405 565 423 -25.0%   4,458 6,409 5,477 -14.5%-------------------------------------------- ----------------------------

DFDS reports monthly ferry volumes for freight and passengers to provideinsightinto the development of volume trends in our European route network enablingtrade and travel in and around Europe. The October 2025 volume report isexpected to be published on 6 November 2025 at around 7.30am CET.

Contact

Torben Carlsen, CEO +45 33 42 32 01

Karen Boesen, CFO +45 20 58 58 40

Søren Brøndholt Nielsen, IR +45 33 42 33 59

Dennis Kjærsgaard, Media +45 42 30 38 47

About DFDS

We operate a transport network in and around Europe with an annual revenue ofDKK 30bn and 16,500 full-time employees.

We move goods in trailers by ferry, road & rail, and we offer complementaryandrelated transport and logistics solutions.

We also move car and foot passengers on short sea and overnight ferry routes.

DFDS was founded in 1866 and headquartered and listed in Copenhagen.

This information is subject to the disclosure requirements pursuant to Section5-12 the Norwegian Securities Trading Act

data.title: REG - Bank of Ireland Grp - Directorate Change
data.description:
RNS Number : 9105C Bank of Ireland Group PLC 10 October 2025  

The Governor and Company of the Bank of Ireland and Bank of Ireland Group plc

(together "Bank of Ireland")

Change in Directorate and Director Responsibilities

10 October 2025

_________________________________________________________________________________

Bank of Ireland announces the appointment of Mr Hans van der Noordaa as an Independent Non-Executive Director to the Board of Bank of Ireland Group plc and the Court of Directors of The Governor and Company of the Bank of Ireland with immediate effect.

Further to the announcement on 9 May regarding Richard Goulding's intended retirement on 31 December 2025, Richard will now step down as an Independent Non-Executive Director on 31 March 2026. This short extension will support the timely completion of succession planning and associated regulatory approvals.

Mr van der Noordaa brings over forty years of leadership experience in financial services, spanning retail and commercial banking, IT, payments, insurance and pensions. His career includes leading transformational change and  significant international exposure across continental Europe and the Asia-Pacific region.

He has held several senior executive roles, including as a member of the Executive Board of ING Group,  a member of the Executive Board of ING Bank and Chief Executive Officer of Delta Lloyd Group plc.  In each of these roles, he also served on the management and supervisory boards. Additionally, he acted as Senior Advisor to the Authority for the Financial Markets in The Netherlands.

Mr van der Noordaa currently holds multiple non-executive positions as Chairman of the supervisory boards of Deloitte Netherlands, War Child, and Johan Cruijff Arena, and serves as an Independent Non-Executive Member of Deloitte's North South Europe Board.

Akshaya Bhargava, Bank of Ireland Chairman, commented: "I'm delighted to welcome Hans to the Board. His deep expertise across banking, insurance, and technology - combined with extensive international and non-executive experience - will bring valuable perspective to our discussions. I look forward to working with him."

This disclosure is made in accordance with Euronext Listing Rule 6.1.5 and London Stock Exchange Listing Rule 9.6.11.

For further information please contact:

Sarah McLaughlin, Group Secretary, +353 87 4315242

data.title: Nomination Committee appointed for Logistea’s Annual General Meeting 2026
data.description:
The Nomination Committee for Logistea AB’s (publ) (“Logistea”) Annual General Meeting 2026 has been appointed in accordance with the instructions of the Nomination Committee adopted by the Annual General Meeting on 9 May 2025.

According to the instructions for the Nomination Committee, the Nomination Committee shall consist of representatives of a maximum of four major shareholders and the Chairman of the Board. The Nomination Committee is appointed by the four largest shareholders in terms of voting rights, appointing one member each to the Nomination Committee. If any of these shareholders do not wish to appoint a member, the subsequent largest shareholders will be given the opportunity to appoint a representative to the Nomination Committee.

The Nomination Committee for the Annual General Meeting 2026 consists of the following members:

  • Rutger Arnhult, appointed by M2 Capital Management AB
  • Gabriel Cronstedt appointed by Nordika Fastigheter
  • Frida Olsson, appointed by The Fourth Swedish National Pension Fund (AP4)
  • Johannes Wingborg, appointed by Länsförsäkringar Fondförvaltning AB
  • Patrik Tillman, Chairman of the Board

Rutger Arnhult has been appointed chairman of the Nomination Committee.

The Nomination Committee together represents approximately 52.3 percent of the total number of shares and 57.8 percent of the total number of votes in Logistea, as per August 31, 2025, taking into account known changes thereafter.

Information about the work of the Nomination Committee is available on Logistea’s website www.logistea.se. Logistea’s shareholders are welcome to submit proposals and views to the Nomination Committee no later than January 31, 2026, by email to nominationcommittee@logistea.se, alternatively by post to Logistea AB (publ), Att. Patrik Tillman, Box 5089, 10242 Stockholm.

The Nomination Committee’s proposal will be published in the notice to the Annual General Meeting 2026 and on the company’s website. The Annual General Meeting of Logistea will be held in Stockholm on Friday May 8, 2026.

data.description:
